english: drying corn. — lċags-šán (lcags shan) iron hoop, hasp, cramp-iron. — lċags-sá (lcags sa) iron ore Cs. — lċags-bsró (lcags bsro) smoothing-iron Sch.

willow, Salix viminalis, almost the only leaved tree in Tibet, frq. planted in the vicinity of villages; rgyál-lċaṅ (rgyal lcang) the specific name of this tree in Kun.; róṅ-lċaṅ (rong lcang), sér-lċaṅ (ser lcang) different species of it; lċaṅ-dkár (lcang dkar) Kun. a white kind with birch-like bark, cf. šo (sho); lċaṅ-ló (lcang lo) willow-leaves, 2. matted hair, lċaṅ-lo-ċan (lcang lo can), or -pa (-pa), one with matted hair, a penitent; also n. of a place in ancient India, of another in Lhasa, and of a third on the top of the fabulous Rirab. 3. queue, pigtail C. — lċaṅ-rlóm (lcang rlom) a flat willow basket Ts. — lċaṅ-šiṅ (lcang shing) willow-tree, willow-wood. — lċaṅ-šól (lcang shol) Sch.: 'the red willow'. — *ċaṅ-síl* (*cang sil*) W. coolness, shade under a willow-tree.
Cs. = saṅ-šóṅ (sang shong) a craggy place, a broken country.

also pyam (phyam), 1. lath, pole, rafter, spar of a roof. In Tibet the rafters are placed horizontally, and support a layer of earth; in Mongol tents they are slanting, supporting the felt-covering. — 2. also brag-lċám (brag lcam), n. of an officinal herb used for healing wounds Med. — 3. gyur-lċám (gyur lcam) prob. denotes a glittering fish, or a fish rapidly darting along — 4. v. lċám-mo (lcam mo).

resp. for spun (spun), and esp. for sríṅ-mo (sring mo), acc. to Cs. also for čuṅ-ma (chung ma), a royal consort, a great man's sister or wife; lha-lċám (lha lcam) a princess Pth.; lċam-čuṅ (lcam chung) a young princess or lady, a young unmarried lady of noble rank; lċam-drál (lcam dral), mċéd-lċam-dral (mched lcam dral), lċam-sríṅ (lcam sring) brother and sister.
1. n. of a flower Wdn.; 2. n. of a kind of vegetables S.g.

1. sbst. (Ld. *lċa* (*lca*), Lh. *ċi-a* (*ci a*), *ċé-a* (*ce a*)), dung, esp. of cattle; bai lċí-ba (ba'i lci ba), bá-lċi (ba lci) cow-dung; lċi-skám (lci skam) dry dung (used as fuel), lċi-rlón (lci rlon) fresh dung. — 2. adj. heavy, W. *ċin-te* (*cin te*), yaṅ lċí (yang lci) 1. light and heavy; 2. weight, *yaṅ-ċí ḍáṅ-ḍa ċó-ċe* (*yang ci dang da co ce*) W. to balance equally, to counterpoise; with regard to food, perh. heavy, oppressing the stomach; but also in a favourable sense: substantial, nutritious; fig.: weighty, important, kyéḍ-kyi skyes daṅ bka-stsál lċí-ba des (khyed kyi skyes dang bka' stsal lci ba des) in consequence of your weighty presents and requests Glr.; *nám-čoq ċín-te* (*nam choq cin te*) W. hard of hearing; ka-na-ma-tó-ba lċí ba (kha na ma tho ba lci ba) a heavy, deadly sin, frq.

denotes a. things, which serve to protect the hands, when having to deal with hot or otherwise disagreeable objects; so gloves may be called lċibs (lcibs) Sch., but esp. tsa-lċíbs (tsha lcibs) (W. *tsalċíb* (*tsalcib*)) pot-cloth (to take pots from the fire), *rę-ċíb* (*re cib*) C., also *lag-ċíb* (*lag cib*) id.; hence prob. mig-lċibs (mig lcibs), resp. spyan-lċibs (spyan lcibs) eyelid; mig-gi lċibs-tór (mig gi lcibs thor) sty, wisp in the eye, and perh. from some remote similarity sgo-lċibs (sgo lcibs), sgoi ya-lċibs (sgo'i ya lcibs) the lintel or head-piece of a door; nya-lċibs (nya lcibs) fishgills, Lex. and Cs.; b. contrivances to facilitate the handling of different objects, as: the handles of pots and vessels, the handles, hilts, bows, ears, loops etc. of knives, scissors, pincers and other working-tools.

Cs., mnyen-lċug (mnyen lcug) Lex., flexible, pliant; a supple branch; lċug-lċúg byéd-pa (lcug lcug byed pa) to bend repeatedly Cs.; lċug-ma (lcug ma) a root-shoot of a willow or a poplar-tree, a rod, switch; *ċùg-gu* (*cug gu*) C. the bud of a twig; lċug-prán (lcug phran) a thin branch or twig.
gri-yi lċugs (gri yi lcugs) Lex. w.e.
Med., lċum-tsa (lcum tsa) Cs.: 'a plant, the stalks of which are used as a purga-
